---
title: "Freedom"
format: html
editor: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

```{r}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)
library(readr)
library(readr)
library(stringr)
library(lubridate)
library(dplyr)
library(forcats)
```

**Introduction** This analysis uses the “Freedom in the World” dataset from Freedom House and the United Nations by way of Arthur Cheib. The dataset provides annual country specific information on political rights and civil liberties from 1972 onward. It includes measures of freedom across 195 countries and 15 territories. It is the standard-setting comparative assessment of global political rights and civil liberties.

For this analysis, I focus on civil liberties and political rights as the key drivers of overall freedom. I begin by examining the
relationship between Civil Liberties (CL) and Political Rights (PR) in 2020 to understand how these measures influence a country’s freedom
classification. I then take a broader look over time analyzing how CL and PR have evolved across years to identify trends in global freedom and determine whether countries are becoming more or less free.

**Main Variables** 
country: The name of the country 

year: The year of observation 

CL (Civil Liberties): A score ranging from 1 to 7, where 1 represents the highest level of freedom (most free) and 7
represents the lowest level of freedom 

PR (Political Rights): A score ranging from 1 to 7, where 1 indicates strong political rights and 7
indicates very limited or absent political rights 

Status: The overall classification of a country’s freedom level(Free (F), Partly Free (PF),
or Not Free (NF) )

**Overall Question:** How do civil liberties and political rights vary
across countries and regions, and how has global freedom changed over
time?

```{r}
freedom <- readr::read_csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2022/2022-02-22/freedom.csv')
```

**Cleaning Countries:**

```{r}
freedom_df <- freedom |>
  mutate(
    country = case_when(
      country == "United States of America" ~ "USA",
      country == "Russian Federation" ~ "Russia",
      country == "Viet Nam" ~ "Vietnam",
      country == "Czechia" ~ "Czech Republic",
      country == "Republic of Korea" ~ "South Korea",
      country == "Democratic People's Republic of Korea" ~ "North Korea",
      country == "Iran (Islamic Republic of)" ~ "Iran",
      country == "Syrian Arab Republic" ~ "Syria",
      country == "Congo" ~ "Republic of Congo",
      country == "Cabo Verde" ~ "Cape Verde",
      country == "Eswatini" ~ "Swaziland",
      country == "Lao People's Democratic Republic" ~ "Laos",
      country == "Micronesia (Federated States of)" ~ "Micronesia",
      country == "United Republic of Tanzania" ~ "Tanzania",
      country == "Bolivia (Plurinational State of)" ~ "Bolivia",
      country == "Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of)" ~ "Venezuela",
      country == "Brunei Darussalam" ~ "Brunei",
      country == "Republic of Moldova" ~ "Moldova",
      country == "Antigua and Barbuda" ~ "Antigua and Barbuda",
      TRUE ~ country
    )
  )
```

I used setdiff function to see what countries I was missing on my map
when cleaning.

```{r}
setdiff(freedom_df$country, map_data("world")$region)
```

**Graph #1** For this first graph, the goal was to provide an overview
of global freedom status in the most recent year available in the
dataset, which happens to be 2020. It gives a starting point for
understanding how countries are classified before looking at trends over
time.

How is freedom status distributed across countries in 2020?

```{r}
library(tidyverse)
library(here)
library(ggplot2)
library(plotly)

freedom_2020 <- freedom_df |>
  filter(year == 2020) |>
  mutate(Status = factor(Status,
                         levels = c("F", "PF", "NF"),
                         labels = c("Free", "Partly Free", "Not Free")))

world_map <- map_data("world")

world_joined <- left_join(world_map, freedom_2020, join_by(region == country))

plot1 <- ggplot(world_joined, aes(x = long, y = lat, group = group, fill = Status, label = region)) +
  geom_polygon(colour="white", linewidth=0.1) +
  theme_void() +
  labs(
    title= "Freedom Status by Country(2020)",
    fill="Status"
  ) + 
  scale_fill_viridis_d(
    labels = c("Free", "Partly Free", "Not Free")
  )
ggplotly(plot1, tooltip = "label")
```

**Interpretation:**

From the map, we can clearly see that Free countries are concentrated in
North America, Europe, and Oceania, while Not Free countries are more
common in Africa and parts of the Middle East and Asia. This highlights
strong regional patterns in global freedom where certain areas
consistently experience higher or lower levels of political rights and
civil liberties.

This map is effective because it provides a clear snapshot of freedom
status across countries in 2020. A map would not be as useful for
showing changes over time but it works well here since the goal is to
display a single year overview.

Colour is used to distinguish between the three categories of freedom
status. A qualitative color scale from the viridis palette is used
because the categories Free, Partly Free, and Not Free are distinct
groups and because it is colour blind friendly.

One of the more challenging parts of creating this visualization was
ensuring that country names matched between datasets. Using the setdiff
function helped identify countries that were not properly matched and
these names were adjusted so that all countries could be correctly
displayed on the map.

Finally, converting the plot to an interactive version using ggplotly
adds value by allowing users to hover over each country and view its
specific freedom status. This makes the visualization more engaging and
provides great additional detail.

**Graph #2** More specifically for 2020, What is the distribution of
Civil Liberties by Freedom Status(Not Free, Partly Free, Free)?

```{r}
ggplot(data=freedom_2020, aes(x = CL, fill = Status))   +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.6) +
   scale_fill_viridis_d() +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Civil Liberties by Freedom Status (2020)",
    x = "Civil Liberties Score",
    y = "Density"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

**Interpretation:**

From this plot, we can see clear differences in Civil Liberties scores
across the three freedom statuses in 2020. Not Free countries are mostly
concentrated at higher scores with many around 6 and ranging from about
4 to 7. In contrast, Free countries are concentrated at the lowest
scores, mainly around 1 and ranging from about 1 to 4. Partly Free
countries fall in between, showing a wider spread across the middle
values.

A density plot works well here because it shows the overall distribution
of scores rather than just counts. This makes it easier to see where
most countries are concentrated and how much overlap exists between the
groups.

This visualization is also useful because it sets up a direct comparison
for the next plot. By understanding how Civil Liberties are distributed
across statuses, we can now compare these patterns to Political Rights
and see whether similar trends appear between the two variables.

**Graph #3** Now, following the last plot, What is the distribution of
Political Rights by Freedom Status(Not Free, Partly Free, Free) in 2020?

```{r}
ggplot(data=freedom_2020, aes(x = PR, fill = Status))   +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.6) +
   scale_fill_viridis_d() +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Political Rights by Freedom Status (2020)",
    x = "Political Rights Score",
    y = "Density"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

**Interpretation:**

This plot shows a very similar pattern to the Civil Liberties
distribution. Not Free countries are highly concentrated at the upper
end with a peak around 7 and range between 4 and 7. Free countries are
concentrated at the lower end with a peak around 1 and range between 1
and 4.

Compared to the Civil Liberties plot, the separation between groups is
even more clear here. Not Free countries are more tightly clustered at
the highest scores which suggests that Political Rights may be slightly
more extreme across categories. Overall, both plots show a strong
relationship between freedom status and these two variables with Free
countries consistently having low scores and Not Free countries having
high scores.

**Graph #4** The previous plots are concentrated around 2020 but they do
not show how freedom changes over time. The next step is to look at
whether there were periods with more Free or not Free countries from
1995 to 2020.

How has the number of Free, Partly Free, and Not Free countries changed
globally from 1995 to 2020?

```{r}
freedom_df_2 <- freedom |>
  mutate(country = case_when(
    
    country == "United States of America" ~ "USA",
    country == "Russian Federation" ~ "Russia",
    country == "Viet Nam" ~ "Vietnam",
    country == "Czechia" ~ "Czech Republic",
    country == "Republic of Korea" ~ "South Korea",
    country == "Democratic People's Republic of Korea" ~ "North Korea",
    country == "Iran (Islamic Republic of)" ~ "Iran",
    country == "Syrian Arab Republic" ~ "Syria",
    country == "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland" ~ "UK",
    country == "Congo" ~ "Republic of Congo",
    country == "Cabo Verde" ~ "Cape Verde",
    country == "Eswatini" ~ "Swaziland",
    country == "Lao People's Democratic Republic" ~ "Laos",
    country == "Micronesia (Federated States of)" ~ "Micronesia",
    country == "United Republic of Tanzania" ~ "Tanzania",
    country == "Bolivia (Plurinational State of)" ~ "Bolivia",
    country == "Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of)" ~ "Venezuela",
    country == "Brunei Darussalam" ~ "Brunei",
    country == "Saint Kitts and Nevis" ~ "Saint Kitts",
    country == "Saint Vincent and the Grenadines" ~ "Saint Vincent",
    country == "Trinidad and Tobago" ~ "Trinidad",
    country == "CÃƒÂ´te dÃ¢â‚¬â„¢Ivoire" ~ "Ivory Coast",
    country == "Republic of Moldova" ~ "Moldova",
    country == "Antigua and Barbuda" ~ "Antigua",
   TRUE ~ country),
   Status = factor(Status,
                         levels = c("F", "PF", "NF"),
                         labels = c("Free", "Partly Free", "Not Free")
   
  ))
```

```{r}
status_trend <- freedom_df_2 |>
  group_by(year, Status) |>
  summarise(count = n())
```

How has the number of Free, Partly Free, and Not Free countries changed
globally from 1995 to 2020?

```{r}
ggplot(data=status_trend, aes(x = year, y = count, colour = Status)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  scale_color_viridis_d()+
  labs(
    title = "Global Freedom Trends Over Time(1995–2020)",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Number of Countries"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

**Interpretation:**

From the line plot, the period between 2005 and 2010 stands out as a
strong period for global freedom. During this time, the number of Free
countries is higher while the number of Not Free countries is lower.
Partly Free countries are also relatively higher as well.

After about 2015 to 2020, this pattern begins to shift. The number of
Free countries levels off or slightly declines, while Not Free countries
increase.The line plot is useful because it clearly shows how these
Status' change over time.

A next step is to look at these changes by region. For example,
comparing 2005 to 2010 with 2015 to 2020 using average Civil Liberties
or Political Rights scores. This can demonstrate which regions are
driving these global trends.

**Graph #5**

```{r}
freedom_periods <- freedom_df |>
  mutate(period = case_when(
    year >= 2005 & year <= 2010 ~ "2005-2010",
    year >= 2015 & year <= 2020 ~ "2015-2020"
  )) |>
  filter(!is.na(period))
```

```{r}
cl_region <- freedom_periods |>
  group_by(Region_Name, period) |>
  summarise(avg_CL = mean(CL, na.rm = TRUE))
```

```{r}
plot5 <- ggplot(cl_region, aes(x = Region_Name, y = avg_CL, fill = period, label=avg_CL)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Average Civil Liberties by Region: 2005–2010 vs 2015–2020",
    x = "Region",
    y = "Average Civil Liberties Score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

ggplotly(plot5, tooltip="label")
```

**Interpretation:**

This bar plot compares average Civil Liberties scores by region for 2005
to 2010 and 2015 to 2020. We can see that most regions have higher
scores in 2015 to 2020 which means civil liberties have declined over
time. This supports the earlier trend we saw in the line plot.

Oceania is the only exception where the pattern is not as clear. This
could be due to countries like Papua New Guinea having a larger impact
on the Oceania countries.

The bar plot works well for comparing regions and time periods, but it
is harder to see the exact differences. Adding ggplotly helps with this
as we can hover and see the actual values. Most regions increased by
about 0.15 to 0.25 which shows a consistent decline in freedom.

Now, it is important to check if the same trend appears for Political
Rights in our next plot.

**Graph #6**

```{r}
pr_region <- freedom_periods |>
  group_by(Region_Name, period) |>
  summarise(avg_PR = mean(PR, na.rm = TRUE))
```

```{r}
plot6 <- ggplot(pr_region, aes(x = Region_Name, y = avg_PR, fill = period, label=avg_PR)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Average Political Rights by Region: 2005–2010 vs 2015–2020",
    x = "Region",
    y = "Average Political Rights Score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

ggplotly(plot6, tooltip="label")
```

**Interpretation:**

This bar plot compares average Political Rights scores by region for
2005 to 2010 and 2015 to 2020. Most regions have higher scores in 2015
to 2020 which illustrates political rights have declined over time. This
trend is very similar to what we saw with Civil Liberties.

Oceania is again an exception, where the pattern is less clear and
possibly due to countries like Papua New Guinea affecting the Oceania
average.

The bar plot is good for comparing regions but it is harder to see exact
differences. Again, using ggplotly helps by showing exact values. Most
regions increased by about 0.10 to 0.30 which showed a small decline in
freedom. Asia changed the least, while the Americas and Oceania changed
the most.

Now we can go further and look at how Civil Liberties and Political
Rights change over time for individual countries.

**Graph #7** How do civil liberties and political rights vary across
countries and regions over time ?

```{r}
freedom_long <- freedom_df |>
  pivot_longer(cols = c(CL, PR),
               names_to = "metric",
               values_to = "value")

freedom_filtereddd <- freedom_df |>
  filter(!is.na(CL) & !is.na(PR)) |>
  group_by(country) |>
  filter(n() >= 20)
```

```{r}
library(shiny)
library(tidyverse)


ui <- fluidPage(
  sidebarLayout(
    sidebarPanel(
      selectizeInput("countrychoose", label = "Choose Countries",
                     choices = unique(freedom_long$country),
                     selected = c("United States of America"),
                     multiple = TRUE),
      
      sliderInput("yearchoose", "Select Year:",
                  min = min(freedom_long$year),
                  max = max(freedom_long$year),
                  value = 2020,
                  step = 1),
      
      actionButton("runappbutton", label = "Update Graph")
    ),
    
    mainPanel(
      plotOutput(outputId = "freedomplot")
    )
  )
)

server <- function(input, output, session) {
  
  freedom_react <- eventReactive(input$runappbutton, {
    freedom_long |>
      filter(country %in% input$countrychoose,
             year == input$yearchoose)
  })
  
 output$freedomplot <- renderPlot({
  
  data <- freedom_filtereddd |>
    filter(country %in% input$countrychoose,
           year <= input$yearchoose)
  
  ggplot(data, aes(x = year)) +
    
    geom_line(aes(y = CL, color = "Civil Liberties", group = country),
              linewidth = 1) +
    
    geom_line(aes(y = PR, color = "Political Rights", group = country),
              linewidth = 1, linetype="dashed") +
    
    scale_y_reverse() +
    
    labs(
      title = glue::glue("Freedom Trends up to {input$yearchoose}"),
      x = "Year",
      y = "Score"
    ) +
    
    theme_minimal()
})
}

shinyApp(ui, server)
```

**Interpretation:** This Shiny app allows users to explore how Civil Liberties and Political Rights change over time for selected countries. It shows both measures on a line plot which helps illustrate and compare trends. It is useful because it allows for both broad comparisons and more detailed exploration between countries as well as between Political Rights and Civil Liberties.

**Conclusion:** In this analysis, examining global freedom trends helped illustrate how freedom status has changed across countries and regions from 1995 to 2020. The graphs show that Free countries are mostly in North America, Europe and Oceania while Not Free countries are more common in Africa and parts of the Middle East and Asia. The results suggest that global freedom was at its highest between 2005 and 2010, followed by a slight decline in more recent years. Both Civil Liberties and Political Rights follow very similar patterns. Both variables have higher scores in recent years,suggesting a decrease in overall freedom. However, these results may be affected by how freedom is measured and the limits of the dataset. If I had more time, I could look deeper into specific regions or countries to better understand what is specifically causing these changes over time.

**Limitations:** The first limitation of this dataset is bias since it is based on how freedom is defined and measured by the organization. Different groups may view political rights and civil liberties differently.

Second, simplifying complex political situations into scores may not fully capture what is happening within a country. 

Lastly, some countries may have missing or inconsistent data across the years whcih can effect comparisons across regions. Smaller regions can also be influenced heavily by one or two countries.

*Future Research:* As mentioned previously, given more time, I would further explore specific countries or regions to better understand how their freedom scores change over time. This could help highlight patterns such as which countries are consistently improving or declining. I could also compare countries within the same region to see how their trends differ and identify any unusual patterns in the data.


**AI Prompts**
1. Help me find countries in my data that do not match the map (Used for Polygon Map)
